Why this is not here?

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The full picture is now clear.

A and B cards are Belgian residence permits, not visas. An A card is a limited-duration permit. A B card is a permanent one. The system only shows these two because Phase 2 is deliberately simplified. It currently supports only three categories: EU citizen, Non-EU worker, and Student. The Non-EU worker policy accepts only A or B cards.

Other permit types like CCard and Annex15 exist but serve different categories. The interface filters these by category.

Visas are entry documents. They do not authorize legal stay. Belgian law requires a residence title for registration. Tourist visas alone do not qualify.

Phase 2 deliberately narrowed the scope from the full specification. The document type enum does not yet include visa codes. In practice, a non-EU worker may enter with a Visa D and later obtain an A card after registration. Blue Card holders would use an H card, though that category is not yet in the interface.
</details></think>

</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Your confusion is fair — the app is showing **Belgian residence card types**, not **entry visas**, and Phase 2 only implements a small slice of the full immigration picture.

## What “A card” and “B card” mean

In Belgium (simplified), these are **residence permits** for non-EU nationals — the plastic card that proves you may **legally stay** in the country, not the sticker in your passport that got you in.

| Card | Meaning (simplified) |
|------|----------------------|
| **A card** | Limited-duration residence permit (renewable, tied to a purpose like work) |
| **B card** | Unlimited / permanent residence permit |

The project glossary documents this:

```38:39:docs/GLOSSARY.md
| **A Card** | Limited-duration residence permit (non-EU) |
| **B Card** | Unlimited-duration residence permit (non-EU) |
```

So when you pick **Non-EU worker**, the next step is not “what visa do they have?” but “what **residence title** do they hold that allows them to live and register here?” For a worker on a standard employment stay, that is typically an **A card** (temporary) or eventually a **B card** (settled).

The UI only offers those two because the code explicitly limits non-EU workers to them:

```275:282:src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Features/Registration/Components/ResidenceStep.razor
    private static IEnumerable<ResidencePermitType> PermitTypesFor(ResidenceCategory category) =>
        category switch
        {
            ResidenceCategory.NonEuWorker =>
            [
                ResidencePermitType.ACard,
                ResidencePermitType.BCard,
            ],
```

And the domain policy enforces the same rule:

```15:18:src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Domain/Immigration/Policies/NonEuWorkerPolicy.cs
        if (context.Permit.PermitType is not (ResidencePermitType.ACard or ResidencePermitType.BCard))
        {
            return ResidencePolicyResult.Invalid(
                "Non-EU workers must hold an A or B card.");
```

## Why you don’t see tourist visa, long-term visa, etc.

**Visas and residence cards are different things** in Belgian practice:

- **Visa C** — short-stay (tourist, business visit). You are a **visitor**, not someone establishing legal residence. Municipalities generally do **not** register you in the population register on a tourist visa.
- **Visa D** — long-stay visa. Often a **step on the way** to a residence card: you enter Belgium, then complete registration and receive an A card (or Annex document, depending on the procedure).
- **A / B card** — the actual **right to reside** that the municipality checks when establishing “legal residence” for registration.

So the app’s **Non-EU worker** path assumes: *this person is here to live and work, and already has (or is being registered with) a proper residence title* — not that they are a tourist passing through.

The full vision in `IDEA.md` does list visas alongside many other documents:

```129:131:IDEA.md
Visa D

Visa C
```

…but that is the **target** workflow. What is built today (Phase 2) is intentionally minimal:

- Only **3** residence categories: EU citizen, Non-EU worker, Student
- Only **5** permit types in the enum (`ACard`, `BCard`, `CCard`, `Annex15`, `EuRegistrationCertificate`)
- No `VisaC` / `VisaD` in `DocumentType` yet (upload types are still just Passport, NationalIdCard, BirthCertificate, ResidencePermit, RentalContract, Other)
- Phase 2’s own doc says the goal was a **demo path**: pick Non-EU worker → record a B card → checklist passes

Many categories from the glossary (researcher, family reunification, highly skilled / H card, refugee, diplomat, etc.) exist in documentation but **not** in the `ResidenceCategory` enum yet — only three values are implemented.

## What this means in practice for your scenario

| Situation | Would it appear in this app today? |
|-----------|-------------------------------------|
| Non-EU worker with A or B card | Yes — this is what the flow is built for |
| Person with only a **tourist visa** (Visa C) | No — wrong workflow; they shouldn’t be registering as a resident |
| Person who just arrived on **Visa D**, card not yet issued | Partially — real life often uses Annex documents or a pending procedure; the app doesn’t model that yet |
| Blue Card / highly skilled worker (H card) | No separate category yet — would need `HighlySkilledWorker` + H card rules |
| Student | Yes — but permit options are **Annex 15** or **B card**, not visas |

## Why the UI feels unclear

Two separate gaps:

1. **Legal simplification** — the app collapses “all non-EU immigration paperwork” into “pick A or B card,” which is accurate only for a narrow happy path.
2. **UX gap** — labels say “A card” / “B card” with no inline explanation (limited vs unlimited permit), so they read like arbitrary codes.

The roadmap acknowledges deeper immigration rules are still future work — Phase 9 lists **“EU vs non-EU policy differences (deepen)”** as high priority, which would be where visas, annexes, H card, refugee paths, etc. would likely get proper modeling.

---

**Bottom line:** You’re not missing a hidden visa dropdown — visas were never implemented in this phase. The app models **residence category + residence permit card**, not the full entry-to-stay journey. For a real municipality, an officer would first ask *why* the person is in Belgium, then check whether they hold a **registerable residence title** (often A card for workers), which is a different question from “what visa stamp is in their passport.”

If you want to go further, sensible next steps would be: add helper text for A/B in the UI, introduce a “pending registration / Visa D only” branch, or add more categories (e.g. highly skilled worker with H card) — but that would be new domain work beyond Phase 2. I can outline what that would look like if you want to plan the next phase.

---

_**User**_

Are you sure it's A and B card ? I thought it was E Card for european; and F for foreigner. 

But to understand, the app right now can register non-eu worker that were already in Belgium before and already have the right to reside in Belgium?

The app doesn't manage someone who doesn't have the right to leave here yet, right?

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user questions whether the correct cards are A and B rather than E and F.

E cards are for EU citizens. F cards are for their family members. A and B cards are for non-EU nationals. H cards are for the European Blue Card.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Good questions — I'll verify how EU vs non-EU cards are modeled in the app and what registration assumes about existing residence rights.

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I now understand the user's question about the card types and the registration scope for non-EU workers already residing in Belgium.

The user's assumption about E and F cards is incorrect. E cards belong to EU citizens exercising freedom of movement. F cards are specifically for family members of EU or Belgian citizens, not for generic non-EU workers. A and B cards handle general non-EU residence permits.

EU citizens pass through automatically without needing a permit. The app does not grant residency rights; it only verifies that non-EU workers already hold a valid A or B card. LegalResidenceEstablished is set to true only after this evidence is recorded.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

You're right to question the card letters — Belgian immigration uses **several** card types, and **E/F are not “European vs foreigner”** in the way people often assume.

## E and F are real — but they mean something different

In Belgium (simplified; cards were renamed in 2021):

| Card | Who it's for |
|------|----------------|
| **EU / EU+** (formerly **E / E+**) | EU citizens exercising freedom of movement — registration certificate |
| **F / F+** | **Family member** of an EU or Belgian citizen — not “any foreigner” |
| **A** | Non-EU national with a **limited-duration** residence permit (e.g. worker) |
| **B** | Non-EU national with **unlimited / permanent** residence |
| **H** | European Blue Card (highly skilled worker) |

So your intuition that **E relates to Europeans** is partly right — but **F is not the generic “foreigner” card**. A non-EU worker on a standard employment permit typically holds an **A card** (or eventually a **B card**), not an F card. F is a specific family-reunification category.

The project's glossary matches this:

```40:41:docs/GLOSSARY.md
| **EU / EU+ Card** | Registration certificate / permanent residence for EU citizens (formerly E / E+) |
| **F / F+ Card** | Family member of an EU or Belgian citizen (F+ = permanent) |
```

For **Non-EU worker**, the app chose A/B because that policy models a standard employment-based stay — not because those are the only non-EU cards that exist in Belgian law.

## What the app actually does per category

| Category | Permit required? | What passes |
|----------|------------------|-------------|
| **EU citizen** | No | Policy passes immediately — no card recorded |
| **Non-EU worker** | Yes | Valid, non-expired **A or B card** |
| **Student** | Yes | Valid **Annex 15** or **B card** |

`EuRegistrationCertificate` exists in the enum but EU citizens don't need to record anything — `EuCitizenPolicy` always returns valid:

```7:10:src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Domain/Immigration/Policies/EuCitizenPolicy.cs
    public ResidencePolicyResult Validate(ResidenceValidationContext context)
    {
        return ResidencePolicyResult.Valid();
    }
```

There is no **F card** path, no **family reunification** category, and no **H card / Blue Card** category in the UI yet — only three residence categories are implemented.

---

## Your second question: does the app only handle people who already have the right to reside?

**Yes — for non-EU cases, that's essentially correct.**

The app does **not** grant immigration status. It **checks** that evidence on file matches a simplified policy, then sets the checklist flag `LegalResidenceEstablished`.

For a non-EU worker that means:

1. Officer selects **Non-EU worker**
2. Officer records permit details (type A or B, dates, card number, etc.)
3. Policy validates: permit exists, type is A or B, `ValidUntil` is not in the past
4. Only then → **Legal residence ✓**

So the happy path assumes: *this person already has a registerable residence title* (A or B card in hand, still valid). The app records and validates that fact; it does not run an immigration application.

## What the app does **not** manage (yet)

| Scenario | Handled today? |
|----------|----------------|
| Tourist on Visa C only | No — no category, no visa types |
| Just arrived on Visa D, card not issued yet | No — no “pending procedure” branch |
| Person with no legal stay / illegal presence | No — roadmap Phase 9 mentions “Illegal stay → reject + refer” as **future** |
| Family reunification (F card) | No — category not implemented |
| Blue Card worker (H card) | No — `HighlySkilledWorker` not in enum |
| Municipality helps you **obtain** your first card as part of registration | No — real life sometimes works that way; this app skips it |

The optional **“Immigration decision”** field is a stub external reference — it doesn't drive whether someone may register.

---

## Putting it together

**Real Belgian workflow (simplified):**  
Arrive → (maybe Visa D) → immigration procedure → receive residence title (A, F, H, Annex, etc.) → **then** municipality registers you in the population register.

**This app today (simplified):**  
Identity → “why are you here?” → **if non-EU, show me you already have A/B (or Annex 15 for students)** → address → police check → …

So you're reading it correctly: the app is built for the **“already legally staying, now registering domicile”** slice, not the **“I need Belgium to give me the right to live here first”** slice. EU citizens get an even lighter path — no permit recorded at all.

If you want, we can sketch what a “Visa D / pending card” or “F card / family reunification” phase would look like in domain terms before implementing it.

---

_**User**_

In the future; we could implement all those kind of workflows ? 

---

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: /Users/joba/Projects/Private/dotnet/SchaerbeekMunicipality/docs/GLOSSARY.md</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user asks whether all those workflows could be implemented in the future.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Yes — and that’s how the project is meant to grow. Phase 2 is a **thin first slice**, not the ceiling.

## The architecture is built for this

The domain is designed around **one policy class per residence category**, plugged in through `IResidencePolicy`:

```140:146:docs/DOMAIN.md
public interface IResidencePolicy
{
    ResidenceCategory Category { get; }
    ValidationResult ValidatePermitDocuments(IReadOnlyList<DocumentReference> docs);
}
```

Today you have three policies (`EuCitizen`, `NonEuWorker`, `Student`). Adding more workflows is mostly:

1. **Extend** `ResidenceCategory` and `ResidencePermitType` (and `DocumentType` for visas, annexes, etc.)
2. **Add** a new policy class (e.g. `FamilyReunificationPolicy` → F card, `HighlySkilledWorkerPolicy` → H card)
3. **Wire** permit options in `ResidenceStep.razor` (`PermitTypesFor`)
4. **Add** tests + a phase doc slice

The checklist model (ADR-0004) also supports this: new paths don’t need a new linear pipeline — they set or clear `LegalResidenceEstablished` via policy, and edge cases can use `Rejected` or `Suspended`.

## What’s already on the roadmap

**Phase 9 — Exception scenarios** explicitly plans deeper immigration paths:

| Planned slice | Relevance |
|---------------|-----------|
| **EU vs non-EU policy differences (deepen)** | High — visas, annexes, more card types, richer rules |
| **Illegal stay → reject + refer** | Person without right to reside |
| **Refugee / temporary protection** | Protection-status paths |
| **Diplomat separate rules** | Special category |

Each is described as: *domain rules + UI branch + dedicated tests* — incremental, not one monolithic rewrite.

## What the full vision already names (not all built yet)

`IDEA.md` and `GLOSSARY.md` already list the categories and documents you’ve been asking about:

- Residence categories: researcher, family reunification, highly skilled worker, diplomat, refugee, cross-border worker, …
- Documents: Visa C/D, Annex 15/19/20/25/26/35, F/H/K/L cards, etc.

Those are **documented targets**; only 3 categories and a handful of permit types are implemented so far.

## What “implementing all workflows” would look like in practice

Not one giant phase — **many small slices**, for example:

| Workflow | Likely additions |
|----------|------------------|
| **Family reunification (F card)** | `FamilyReunification` category + `FCard` permit type + policy |
| **Blue Card worker (H card)** | `HighlySkilledWorker` category (separate from generic `NonEuWorker`) |
| **Visa D, card pending** | `DocumentType.VisaD`, policy branch: “annex / decision OK while card pending” |
| **Tourist / no right to stay** | No registration path → `Reject` with reason, refer elsewhere |
| **EU citizen with E/EU card** | Optional `EuRegistrationCertificate` recording (today EU passes with no permit) |
| **Refugee / subsidiary protection** | New categories + annex-based policies |

The optional **Immigration decision** stub is also a hook for “waiting on DVZ/IBZ” scenarios later.

## Honest limits

This is an **educational** project — the glossary even says rules are simplified. “All workflows” in the real Belgian sense would mean:

- Dozens of card/annex combinations with different legal consequences  
- Interactions with immigration office systems (not just a reference field)  
- Edge cases (marriage not recognised, duplicate identity, etc. — some planned in Phase 9)

So: **yes, you can grow toward full coverage**, but **incrementally**, with explicit simplified policies — not a production legal engine on day one.

---

**Short answer:** Yes. The app today is “non-EU worker already has A/B card → register domicile.” The codebase and roadmap are set up to add F card, H card, visas, pending procedures, reject-illegal-stay, refugee paths, and the rest — **one category/policy slice at a time**, which matches how municipalities actually handle distinct cases.

If you want to prioritise what to build next, **family reunification (F)** and **deepening EU/non-EU rules (visas + annexes)** are the most natural follow-ons to what you’ve been exploring.
